text: The surgeon applies traction to expose the surgical field, identifying the pubic bone as the first landmark. They proceed laterally along the umbilical artery, opening it up to reveal the vas deferens, which is clearly visible and can be safely dissected.
The surgeon drops the bladder completely, preparing for further dissection steps in the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon carefully exposes the endopelvic fascia, preparing to incise it to better delineate the border of the prostate. The bladder is emptied again to provide more space in the surgical field. Periprosthetic fat is removed and sent for final pathology analysis.

The surgeon carefully isolates the dorsal vein, avoiding distal movement to prevent retraction and bleeding. The surgical field reveals the border of the bladder and a small prostate, with visible periprostatic fat.
The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia, applying traction to identify the optimal plane for nerve sparing. A minor incision is made to confirm the correct entry point.
The surgeon gently pushes the muscle fibers aside to reveal the shape and distal end of the small prostate, carefully assessing its boundaries.

The surgeon applies traction and uses a Maryland dissector to spread tissue on the opposite side, allowing for deeper dissection without nerve sparing. The focus is on the arterial part where cancer is suspected.

The surgeon makes an incision through the endopelvic fascia, exposing the levator fibers. The base of the prostate is identified, and traction is applied to facilitate opening the bladder. The detrusor muscle fibers are visible, guiding the entry point into the bladder neck.
The surgeon deflates the balloon and opens the bladder, then pulls the catheter slightly back to grab the tip and pushes it in again.

The surgeon uses a force arm to lift the bladder, revealing the bladder neck and following the shape of the prostate. Care is taken not to thin the bladder wall excessively, while observing the distance to the orifices inside.
The surgeon applies traction to reveal the detrusor fibers, using the ProGrasp instrument to manipulate the catheter. The detrusor fibers become visible as they separate from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully navigates to avoid entering the prostate, confirming the correct path by double-checking the bladder wall thickness. Urine is drained as the bladder is gently pushed. The surgeon identifies a bleeder and applies suction while using the Maryland forceps for counter traction, revealing visible fibers.

The surgeon continues the dissection, identifying and isolating the vas deferens. Two hemoclips are placed on the bladder neck to open the space further. The perivesical fat and prostate are visible, with care taken not to place the hemoclips too deeply to avoid an open vessel.

The surgeon exposes the seminal vesicle and cuts it, noting the absence of vessels in the area. The left vas is visible, and suction is applied to follow the vas further down, ensuring there is no bleeding.

The surgeon clips a larger vessel near the seminal vesicle using a hemolog, ensuring it is not placed too close to the bladder neck. The denovus fascia covering the seminal vesicle is visible, and the surgeon pushes it to the midline to identify the plane of the fascia. The vessel is isolated and taken into the hemolog.

The surgeon carefully follows the seminal vesicle downwards, applying small clips to larger vessels for safety. The edge of the Denonvilliers fascia is visible, indicating the correct anatomical plane. The fascia is peeled off from the seminal vesicle to better identify the structure.

The surgeon lifts the prostate slightly and uses scissors to perform a blunt dissection, aiming for an intrafascial approach on the right side. The periprostatic fat is visible, and the dissection is carefully followed to maintain the correct plane.

The surgeon identifies the important anatomical plane beneath the prostate, noting some oozing and placing a small blue clip to control it. The prostate is released at the base, with preparations made for further clipping.

The surgeon carefully identifies and manipulates the lateral fascia covering the nerves, avoiding the nursery while pushing the fascia away. A clear window is visible, though the area is sticky, prompting a change in approach to dissect the fibers and identify small vessels. The position of the prostate is adjusted periodically to facilitate access.

The surgeon identifies the capsule of the prostate and pushes surrounding fibers away, attempting to follow the surgical plane from the posterior approach. The prostate is repositioned slightly to facilitate better access and visibility.

The surgeon is in the correct plane and proceeds with dissection using a posterior approach, placing a blue clip to control bleeding. The prostate is repositioned slightly, and the dissection continues anteriorly while maintaining control over the surgical plane.
The surgeon approaches the apex of the prostate, an important part of the prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon visualizes the apex of the prostate and gently pushes the neurovascular bundles away to expose the capsule. A clip is placed near the anastomosis site, with the urethra visible starting at the apex. The surgeon checks the midline to ensure proper treatment of the prostate on the rectal side, using scissors to gently push and open the tissue without rupturing it.

The surgeon performs a non-nerve sparing procedure on one side, using hemolog clips to secure tissue. The surgical plane is identified, and electrocautery is used to dissect through fatty tissue. The prostatectomy is performed before proceeding to lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon uses an extra-facial approach, navigating through fatty tissue and identifying the denovus fascia. A clip is placed on the tissue, and the surgeon carefully maneuvers around an artery while leaving some tissue in the fossa.

The surgeon has removed the prostate, revealing the prosthetic fossa. Hemolog clips are applied to secure tissue margins on the left and right sides. The camera is cleaned to improve visibility, and the surgical field is adjusted to approach the apex, with visible fatty tissue surrounding the area.
The surgeon inserts a catheter to visualize its tip, revealing the position of fibers around the prostate. Vessels are visible near the prostate, which is grasped using a Maryland forceps.

The surgeon carefully develops the apex of the prostate, entering the centaurine fascia and plexus. The visual focus is on the border between the sphincter muscle and the apex, ensuring no bleeding occurs during this precise dissection.

The surgeon identifies the correct plane for dissection near the urethra, moving slightly away from the apex. Muscle fibers appear whitish, marking the border to the prostate. The surgeon plans to remove the tissue at the end of the prostate.

The surgeon navigates down to the catheter, encountering minor arterial bleeding. The bleeding is controlled, and the Santorini's plexus is visible, with discussions about potentially placing stitches without altering the anatomy.

The surgeon frees the prostate and proceeds through the neurovascular bundle using cautery, as preservation is not required in this area. The prostate is then taken, revealing the muscle and rectolateral aspect. Pure fat is visible with no nerves present.

The surgeon cuts through the apical region and basis of the prostate, focusing on the urethra, seminal vesicle, and attached nerves. The inner side of the tissue is yellow, aiding in orientation for pathological examination. The surgeon discusses the importance of defining margins, noting the apical region and anterior side for precise pathological assessment.
The surgeon prepares to suture the dorsal vein complex using Vicryl sutures, ensuring the colors on the specimen do not wipe off during handling.
The surgeon places the first suture in the distal urethra, locking it securely in place.
The surgeon prepares to perform the Rocco suture, a modified technique, noting minimal bleeding in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a monofilic monocryal suture, approximately 18-20 centimeters long, to perform a two-layer Rocco suture. The first layer moves the bladder down, while the second layer grabs the mucosa. There is some bleeding, which is addressed with pinpoint cautery. The posterior layer is grabbed with a good bite, and the ilial conduit is mobilized. The right urethral dissection is prepared with a handmade sling.

The surgeon manipulates the Denonvilliers fascia, taking a substantial bite of tissue beneath the urethra using a Maryland dissector. The fascia is moved downward, and the surgeon considers using a clip to secure it, noting the potential for the suture to slide back.
The surgeon applies a clip to secure the border of the Denoviae fascia, reducing the surgical field size and stopping minor oozing.

The surgeon inspects the surgical field for bleeding and identifies a small vessel that requires clipping. A small clip is applied to the vessel to control bleeding before proceeding with the second layer of the Rocco suture.

The surgeon manipulates the bladder neck, which has a small opening, aiming to grasp the edge of the mucosa. The mucosa is visible and retracted, and the surgeon lifts the catheter slightly to improve access in the narrow pelvic space, encountering some collision with the pelvis.

The surgeon addresses minor oozing in the surgical field while performing suturing. The mucosa is visible, and the surgeon carefully adjusts the catheter to avoid closing the space too much. Using one arm, the surgeon holds the thread to ensure the mucosa edges are touching without rupturing the tissue.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to tie a knot, ensuring good resistance in the midline of the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully sutures the complex, deliberately avoiding incorporation of the sphincter muscle into the suture line to preserve its functional tissue. The strong suture line is visible, with the muscle spared from the anastomosis.
The surgeon examines the mucosa and bladder neck, avoiding vascular structures and the sutured complex on this side. The procedure will be repeated on the anterior side.

The surgeon grasps the sutured dorsal vein complex, highlighting the muscle tissue in the area. The robotic approach facilitates easier access to the neurovascular bundle and allows for the completion of the anastomosis, which is challenging in open surgeries. The final catheter is placed.

The surgeon fills the bladder with a syringe, noting the presence of residual urine due to a small bladder neck. After adding approximately 200 milliliters, a leakage is observed on the lateral side, indicated by visible bubbles. The catheter is in place and blocked, and the bladder is emptied to create space for lymph node access.

The surgeon identifies a leakage and applies a suture to address it, adjusting the length of the thread for optimal closure. Tissue manipulation is visible as the surgeon prepares to redo the cystogram if necessary.
The surgeon ties a suture around a small vessel, ensuring it is secure. Scissors are introduced via robotic arm three to proceed with lymph node dissection.
The surgeon follows the dissection down to the ureter, opening the tissue further. Two hemlock clips are applied to secure vessels, while handling accompanying vessels with care.
The surgeon identifies the urethra, gonadal vein, and thickened lymphatic tissue, with the epigastric vessels visible in the upper part of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and proceeds to open the surrounding tissue further, revealing the anatomical structure more clearly.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and cautions against grabbing it, highlighting its proximity to the internal iliac vessels.

The surgeon pushes the ureter aside to improve exposure, revealing lymph nodes for removal. The genital femoral nerve serves as a landmark, with careful dissection avoiding lateral movement from this point.
The surgeon identifies an enlarged lymph node in the surgical field, carefully maneuvering around it during the lymphadenectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies a small clip to a vessel running parallel in the surgical field, assessing whether additional clips are necessary due to the vessel's small size.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers to remove tissue from the trocar, ensuring it is securely grasped at the end. The surgical field is checked for bleeding, particularly along the side wall and upper area, before inspecting the artery.
The surgeon inspects the area underneath the external structure for any remaining tissue, ensuring thorough removal. The surgeon then moves to the left side to continue the procedure.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum to expose the vas deferens and identifies the adjacent nerve and the edge of the vein.
The surgeon manipulates the tissue to free it further, revealing the ureter, which appears slim with minimal surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery and prepares to manipulate it, ensuring careful handling to avoid coagulation, with the bowel visible underneath.
The surgeon identifies a vessel and prepares to manipulate it, ensuring it remains visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a nerve and carefully monitors the entry point of a vein while proceeding with the dissection.
